name: Forecasting & Time Series
tagline: Predict the future from historical patterns
description: Forecasting & Time Series is one of the most commercially impactful AI sub-fields, spanning classical statistical methods (ARIMA, ETS), machine learning ensembles (LightGBM, Random Forests), deep learning architectures (Transformers, N-BEATS, PatchTST), and foundation models (TimesFM, Chronos, Moirai) that enable zero-shot cold-start forecasting without per-dataset retraining. The domain is foundational to demand planning, inventory optimisation, logistics capacity, energy grid balancing, and financial risk management across every major industry vertical.

core_concepts.name: Stationarity & Differencing
core_concepts.definition: A time series is weakly stationary if its mean, variance, and autocovariance are constant over time. Non-stationary series are transformed via differencing (removing trends) or log/Box-Cox transforms (stabilising variance) before classical modelling. The Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test is the standard stationarity diagnostic.
core_concepts.significance: Violating stationarity is the single most common error in classical time series modelling. Automated tools (AutoARIMA, StatsForecast) perform stationarity testing internally, but practitioners must understand the diagnostics to interpret model failures.

core_concepts.name: Decomposition (Trend, Seasonality, Residual)
core_concepts.definition: A time series is decomposed into structural components — trend (T), seasonality (S), and residual (R). Additive decomposition assumes these sum; multiplicative assumes they multiply. STL (Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess) is robust to changing seasonality and outliers. MSTL extends STL to multiple seasonality periods.
core_concepts.formula_latex: \text{Additive: } y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t; \quad \text{Multiplicative: } y_t = T_t \times S_t \times R_t
core_concepts.significance: Decomposition provides interpretability that pure ML lacks. Trend and seasonality removal is a preprocessing step for many models. Identifying irregular or shifting seasonality directly informs model selection.

core_concepts.name: ARIMA & Autoregressive Models
core_concepts.definition: ARIMA(p,d,q) combines autoregression (AR — linear combination of p past values), integration (I — d-th order differencing), and moving average (MA — q past forecast errors). SARIMA adds seasonal counterparts. SARIMAX incorporates exogenous variables. Box-Jenkins methodology provides the systematic order identification and diagnostic checking workflow.
core_concepts.significance: ARIMA remains a strong, interpretable benchmark and baseline. AutoARIMA via StatsForecast fits thousands of series in seconds, making it competitive at scale.

core_concepts.name: Exponential Smoothing & ETS Models
core_concepts.definition: Exponential smoothing assigns exponentially decreasing weights to past observations. The ETS (Error-Trend-Seasonality) framework unifies all variants under a probabilistic state space formulation with 30 possible model combinations. Model selection via AIC. Holt-Winters handles multiplicative seasonality. Auto-ETS automates selection.
core_concepts.significance: ETS is the gold standard for univariate business forecasting with few parameters and strong empirical performance. StatsForecast AutoETS processes 10,000 series in seconds.

core_concepts.name: LSTM & Recurrent Neural Networks
core_concepts.definition: LSTMs address the vanishing gradient problem of vanilla RNNs via a cell state and three gates (input, forget, output) that regulate long-term information flow. GRUs are a simplified two-gate variant. TCNs use dilated causal convolutions for parallel training and explicit receptive field control.
core_concepts.formula_latex: c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t; \quad h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(c_t)
core_concepts.significance: LSTMs introduced the deep learning era for time series and powered production systems at Google, Amazon, and Uber from 2016–2021. Understanding them is prerequisite for Transformer architectures.

core_concepts.name: Transformer-Based Forecasting & Patch Tokenisation
core_concepts.definition: Transformers model dependencies between any two timesteps via scaled dot-product attention. Direct application to raw timesteps proved problematic. PatchTST treats non-overlapping time patches as tokens (reducing sequence length by factor P). iTransformer inverts attention to operate across variates (features) rather than time, capturing multivariate correlations.
core_concepts.significance: Transformers dominate long-horizon forecasting leaderboards. PatchTST and iTransformer are current best practice for production multivariate forecasting. The patch design principle transfers to foundation model architecture.

core_concepts.name: Probabilistic Forecasting & Prediction Intervals
core_concepts.definition: Probabilistic forecasting produces a full predictive distribution (quantiles, sample paths) rather than a single point estimate. Key methods: conformal prediction (approximate coverage under mild stationarity conditions), quantile regression (minimise pinball loss), DeepAR (global LSTM with parametric likelihood), and diffusion models (diverse trajectory generation). CRPS and Winkler Score are proper scoring rules for evaluation.
core_concepts.significance: Supply chain safety stock, capacity buffers, and financial hedging decisions require uncertainty quantification. Conformal prediction provides approximate (not guaranteed) coverage for time series — the exchangeability assumption does not strictly hold and rolling re-calibration is required.

core_concepts.name: Global vs. Local Forecasting Models
core_concepts.definition: Local models train independently per series. Global models train jointly on thousands of series, learning shared patterns via cross-series information transfer. Global models are more data-efficient per series and handle cold-start better. Foundation models (TimesFM, Chronos) are the extreme of global models — pre-trained on billions of data points for universal zero-shot inference. Negative transfer risk exists when series are too heterogeneous.
core_concepts.significance: The shift from local to global to foundation models is the central architectural trend 2020–2026. Choosing correctly between tiers directly determines model quality, compute cost, and cold-start handling.

core_concepts.name: Hierarchical Forecasting & MinT Reconciliation
core_concepts.definition: Enterprise forecasting requires coherent forecasts across hierarchy levels (SKU → category → region → national). MinT (Minimum Trace) projects base forecasts onto the coherent subspace by minimising forecast error variance using the shrinkage-estimated covariance matrix W. The summing matrix S encodes the aggregation structure.
core_concepts.formula_latex: \tilde{y}_t = S(S^T W^{-1} S)^{-1} S^T W^{-1} \hat{y}_t; \quad y_t = S b_t
core_concepts.significance: Every enterprise forecasting system deals with hierarchies. Ignoring coherence produces conflicting forecasts across reporting levels. MinT is production-ready in StatsForecast, sktime, and fable. Dynamic hierarchies require versioned summing matrices.

core_concepts.name: Time Series Foundation Models (Zero-Shot Forecasting)
core_concepts.definition: Foundation models for time series are large neural networks pre-trained on massive, diverse corpora (millions of series, multiple domains), enabling zero-shot or few-shot forecasting on unseen series without retraining. TimesFM (Google DeepMind, 200M params, 100B training points), Chronos (Amazon, T5-based tokenisation), Moirai (Salesforce, MoE outputs, LOTSA dataset), and MOMENT (CMU, BERT-style masked pre-training). Zero-shot performance surpasses statistical baselines on cold-start tasks; fine-tuned models outperform on in-distribution data once ≥60 observations are available.
core_concepts.formula_latex: \mathcal{L}_{\text{pretrain}} = -\sum \log p_\theta(\text{patch}_t | \text{patch}_{1:t-1}); \quad \text{Zero-shot: no gradient updates at inference}
core_concepts.significance: Foundation models solve the cold-start problem — the primary barrier to early platform adoption. They complement, not replace, task-specific trained models. The 12–24 month window before hyperscaler commoditisation requires urgent reference client acquisition.

core_concepts.name: Walk-Forward Cross-Validation with Gap
core_concepts.definition: Time series cross-validation uses expanding training windows with a gap between the training end and validation start. The gap must equal max(forecast_horizon, max_lag_used_in_features) to prevent future data from bleeding into training-time features. Incorrect gap configuration produces optimistic training metrics (MASE ~0.78) that degrade silently in production (~0.95).
core_concepts.significance: The #1 source of production accuracy degradation in forecasting systems. Documented in 30–50% of ML forecasting post-mortems. Must be a first-class CI/CD blocking gate, not a best-practice footnote.

core_concepts.name: Evaluation Metrics for Forecasting
core_concepts.definition: MAE (interpretable, same units), RMSE (penalises outliers), MAPE (avoid when actuals near zero), SMAPE (bounded alternative), MASE (scale-free, comparable across series — primary production metric). For probabilistic: Winkler Score (interval width + coverage penalty) and CRPS (proper scoring rule for full distributions). MASE < 1.0 vs seasonal naive is the standard deployment gate.
core_concepts.significance: Metric choice affects which model appears to win. MASE is the recommended primary metric because it is scale-free, handles seasonality via the naive denominator, and allows meaningful comparison across heterogeneous series portfolios.

algorithms.name: ARIMA / SARIMA
pros: Mathematically well-understood and interpretable parameters
Strong performance on univariate short-horizon forecasting
Produces valid prediction intervals under Gaussian assumptions
AutoARIMA automates order selection; StatsForecast scales to thousands of series
cons: Assumes linearity; cannot capture complex non-linear patterns
Requires stationarity preprocessing
Struggles with irregular or multiple seasonality periods
use_cases: Univariate short-horizon business KPIs
Financial returns and economic indicators
Baseline benchmark for any forecasting system
key_papers: Box & Jenkins (1970). Time Series Analysis: Forecasting and Control. Holden-Day.
Hyndman & Khandakar (2008). Automatic Time Series Forecasting. Journal of Statistical Software, 27(3).

algorithms.name: ETS / Holt-Winters
algorithms.complexity: O(n·m) where m is seasonal period
pros: Few parameters with strong empirical performance
30-model family with AIC-based automatic selection
Prediction intervals from probabilistic state space formulation
StatsForecast AutoETS fits 10,000 series in seconds
cons: Limited to additive/multiplicative combinations of trend and seasonality
Cannot incorporate external covariates natively
Underperforms on hierarchical, covariate-rich use cases vs LightGBM
use_cases: Univariate business KPI forecasting
ERP-embedded demand planning
High-frequency automated forecasting at scale
Fallback model when ML models fail
key_papers: Hyndman et al. (2008). Forecasting with Exponential Smoothing: The State Space Approach. Springer.
M4 Competition (Makridakis et al., 2020). The M4 Competition. IJF.

algorithms.name: LightGBM (Global Model with Lag Features)
algorithms.complexity: O(n·d·log n) where d is number of features
pros: M5 Competition winner — best on real-world hierarchical retail data
Native categorical feature support (store_id, sku_id)
SHAP-based explainability for feature attribution
Fastest training on tabular feature-engineered time series data
Tweedie loss handles zero-inflated demand distributions
cons: Performance gated on feature engineering quality — lag and rolling feature design is critical
Does not capture inter-series correlations natively
Walk-forward CV gap misconfiguration silently degrades production accuracy
use_cases: Retail demand forecasting at SKU×Store level (10K–1M series)
Logistics shipment volume forecasting
Supply chain inventory replenishment
Any tabular time series with ≥50 series and ≥100 rows/series
key_papers: Ke, G. et al. (2017). LightGBM: A Highly Efficient Gradient Boosting Decision Tree. NeurIPS 2017.
M5 Competition (Makridakis et al., 2022). M5 accuracy competition. IJF.

algorithms.name: Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT)
pros: Handles static, known-future, and unknown-future covariates cleanly via variable selection
Quantile outputs for probabilistic forecasting built-in
Attention-based interpretability: which past timesteps drove the forecast
Top-3 on multiple real-world business forecasting benchmarks
cons: Complex architecture with many hyperparameters; brittle to training instability
Requires careful feature separation by input type
Slower to train than MLP-based methods
use_cases: Logistics origin-destination volume forecasting with known future events
Energy load forecasting with weather and calendar covariates
Multi-horizon retail forecasting requiring interpretable attention
key_papers: Lim, B. et al. (2021). Temporal Fusion Transformers for Interpretable Multi-horizon Time Series Forecasting. IJF, 37(4). arXiv:1912.09363

algorithms.name: PatchTST
algorithms.complexity: O(n·(L/P)²) where L is sequence length and P is patch size
pros: Patch tokenisation captures local temporal semantics — strong on long horizons
Channel independence avoids spurious cross-variate noise
Pre-trainable via masked patch modelling (self-supervised)
Outperforms all prior Transformers on 8 standard benchmarks
cons: Does not model cross-variate correlations by design
Requires sufficient training data for supervised training
Not zero-shot without pre-training corpus
use_cases: Long-horizon univariate and multivariate forecasting
Energy, traffic, weather forecasting at high frequency
Transfer learning across domains via masked pre-training
key_papers: Nie, Y. et al. (2023). A Time Series is Worth 64 Words: Long-term Forecasting with Transformers. ICLR 2023. arXiv:2211.14730

algorithms.name: N-BEATS
algorithms.complexity: O(n·L·H) in sequence length and horizon
pros: Purely MLP-based — no recurrence or convolution; fast and parallelisable
Interpretable variant decomposes into trend and seasonality basis expansions
No exogenous variable requirement
Strong benchmark results on M4 competition data
cons: Does not naturally handle multivariate inputs
Requires significant data for global training
Interpretable variant imposes structural assumptions
use_cases: Long-horizon univariate forecasting
Interpretable component decomposition for business users
Competition-grade univariate benchmarks
key_papers: Oreshkin, B.N. et al. (2020). N-BEATS: Neural Basis Expansion Analysis for Interpretable Time Series Forecasting. ICLR 2020. arXiv:1905.10437

algorithms.name: DeepAR
algorithms.complexity: O(n·T·H) in training; sequential at inference
pros: Full predictive distribution — quantiles and sample paths
Global model leverages patterns across thousands of related series
Handles count data (Negative Binomial), continuous data (Gaussian/Student-t)
Handles missing values and irregular time series natively
cons: Autoregressive inference is sequential — slow for long horizons
Training requires large corpora of related series
Less interpretable than statistical methods
use_cases: Probabilistic demand forecasting at product level
Intermittent demand (spare parts, slow-moving SKUs)
Energy consumption forecasting with distributional outputs
key_papers: Salinas, D. et al. (2020). DeepAR: Probabilistic Forecasting with Autoregressive Recurrent Networks. IJF, 36(3). arXiv:1704.04110

algorithms.name: Chronos
algorithms.complexity: O(1) inference (no training required per dataset)
pros: Zero-shot probabilistic forecasting via T5 backbone with quantised token vocabulary
Strong CRPS on 42 benchmark datasets
Multiple model sizes (Tiny to Large) for cost-accuracy tradeoffs
Open-source on HuggingFace — multiple sizes available
cons: Tokenisation quantisation introduces discretisation error
Slower than statistical methods for single series (2s/series CPU for large model)
Underperforms fine-tuned models on in-distribution data
use_cases: Cold-start forecasting for new entities (< 60 observations)
Diverse-domain zero-shot benchmarking
Probabilistic cold-start with uncertainty quantification
key_papers: Ansari, A.F. et al. (2024). Chronos: Learning the Language of Time Series. TMLR 2024. arXiv:2403.07815

algorithms.name: TimesFM
pros: Zero-shot forecasting with 200M parameter decoder-only Transformer
Trained on 100B real-world data points (Google Trends, Wikipedia, synthetic)
Outperforms statistical baselines on zero-shot tasks across 34 datasets
Deployable on standard hardware; pip-installable
cons: Primarily point forecasting (probabilistic extension in progress as of 2025)
Outperformed by fine-tuned models on in-distribution data
First cold-start incurs 30–60s model load time without pre-warming
use_cases: Cold-start forecasting across any domain
Rapid prototyping and PoC forecasting pipelines
Benchmark baseline against which task-specific models are compared
key_papers: Das, A. et al. (2024). A Decoder-Only Foundation Model for Time-Series Forecasting. ICML 2024. arXiv:2310.10688

algorithms.name: Prophet
algorithms.complexity: O(n log n) via Stan MCMC/MAP
pros: Analyst-accessible — no time series expertise required
Automatic changepoint detection with Laplace prior
Built-in holiday effects and custom seasonality periods
Robust to missing data and outliers
cons: Slower at scale than StatsForecast AutoETS for batch processing
Cannot incorporate lag-based ML features natively
Underperforms LightGBM on hierarchical multi-series problems
use_cases: Business series with known holiday effects
Analyst-driven forecasting without ML infrastructure
Series with abrupt level shifts or structural breaks
key_papers: Taylor, S.J. & Letham, B. (2018). Forecasting at Scale. The American Statistician, 72(1). doi:10.1080/00031305.2017.1380080

sota_2024_2026.summary: 2024 was the foundation model year for time series. TimesFM (Google DeepMind, ICML 2024), Chronos (Amazon, TMLR 2024), Moirai (Salesforce, ICML 2024), and MOMENT (CMU, ICML 2024) all released within months of each other. These models enable zero-shot forecasting without per-dataset training. Simultaneously, iTransformer (ICLR 2024) recovered Transformer competitiveness for multivariate series by inverting attention to operate across variates. Mamba-based state space models (linear O(n) complexity) emerged as an architectural alternative. The Gift-Eval benchmark (2024) provided the first holistic multi-domain evaluation showing no single architecture universally dominates.

key_models.name: Chronos
key_models.innovation: Numeric tokenisation via T5, probabilistic outputs

key_models.name: Moirai
key_models.innovation: Masked pre-training, MoE output heads, LOTSA dataset

key_models.name: MOMENT
key_models.innovation: BERT-style masked patches, Time Series Pile (13 domains)

key_models.name: iTransformer
key_models.innovation: Inverted attention across variates instead of time

ongoing_debates: Foundation models vs fine-tuned models: foundation models lead on cold-start but lag on in-distribution (Gift-Eval, 2024)
Transformers vs linear models: DLinear debate (Zeng 2023) partially resolved by PatchTST/iTransformer but linear models remain competitive on many real datasets
Mamba vs Transformer: linear complexity advantage is real but benchmark superiority is not yet decisive

key_papers.title: Time Series Analysis: Forecasting and Control
key_papers.contribution: Introduced the ARIMA model class and the iterative Box-Jenkins 3-step methodology. Established ACF/PACF as diagnostic tools and MLE as the estimation framework. The single most influential work in time series forecasting.

key_papers.title: Forecasting with Exponential Smoothing: The State Space Approach
key_papers.authors: Hyndman, R.J., Koehler, A.B., Ord, J.K., Snyder, R.D.
key_papers.contribution: Unified all exponential smoothing methods under the ETS probabilistic state space framework, enabling AIC-based model selection and proper prediction intervals. Implemented as ets() in R forecast package.

key_papers.title: DeepAR: Probabilistic Forecasting with Autoregressive Recurrent Networks
key_papers.authors: Salinas, D., Flunkert, V., Gasthaus, J., Januschowski, T.
key_papers.contribution: First large-scale global probabilistic forecasting model using LSTM with parametric likelihood. Demonstrated cross-series transfer learning for forecasting. Production-deployed at Amazon.

key_papers.title: N-BEATS: Neural Basis Expansion Analysis for Interpretable Time Series Forecasting
key_papers.authors: Oreshkin, B.N., Carpov, D., Chapados, N., Bengio, Y.
key_papers.contribution: Pure MLP architecture with residual stacking. Interpretable variant uses Fourier and polynomial basis functions for trend/seasonality decomposition. Outperformed all classical methods on M4 competition data.

key_papers.title: Temporal Fusion Transformers for Interpretable Multi-horizon Time Series Forecasting
key_papers.authors: Lim, B., Arık, S.O., Loeff, N., Pfister, T.
key_papers.contribution: Specialised Transformer for forecasting with variable selection networks, gated residual networks, and attention-based interpretability. Handles mixed input types including static, known-future, and observed covariates.

key_papers.title: A Time Series is Worth 64 Words: Long-term Forecasting with Transformers
key_papers.authors: Nie, Y., Nguyen, N.H., Sinthong, P., Kalagnanam, J.
key_papers.contribution: Introduced PatchTST: patch-based tokenisation capturing local temporal context, channel independence reducing noise, and self-supervised pre-training via masked patch modelling. Outperformed all prior Transformers on 8 benchmarks.

key_papers.title: Are Transformers Effective for Time Series Forecasting?
key_papers.authors: Zeng, A., Chen, M., Zhang, L., Liu, Q.
key_papers.contribution: Showed a simple one-layer linear model (DLinear) outperforms multiple Transformer variants on 7/8 standard benchmarks. Triggered major re-evaluation of Transformer-based TS research and raised benchmark design questions.

key_papers.title: A Decoder-Only Foundation Model for Time-Series Forecasting
key_papers.authors: Das, A., Kong, W., Leach, A., Mathur, S., Sen, R., Yu, R.
key_papers.contribution: TimesFM: 200M parameter decoder-only Transformer trained on 100B real-world data points. Zero-shot performance competitive with statistical baselines on 34 datasets. Deployed internally at Google.

key_papers.title: Chronos: Learning the Language of Time Series
key_papers.authors: Ansari, A.F., Stella, L., Türkmen, C., et al. (Amazon)
key_papers.contribution: Tokenised numeric time series via mean-scaling and quantisation into 4096-bin vocabulary, fine-tuning T5 as an autoregressive forecaster. Strong zero-shot CRPS on 42 benchmark datasets. Open-sourced on HuggingFace.

key_papers.title: Unified Training of Universal Time Series Forecasting Transformers
key_papers.authors: Woo, G., Liu, C., Kumar, A., Xiong, C., Savarese, S., Sahoo, D.
key_papers.contribution: Moirai: universal forecasting model using masked encoder pre-training on LOTSA (27B data points). Mixture-of-experts output heads handle different distribution types. Any-variate attention for multivariate series.

key_papers.title: iTransformer: Inverted Transformers Are Effective for Time Series Forecasting
key_papers.authors: Liu, Y., Hu, T., Zhang, H., et al.
key_papers.contribution: Inverted Transformer applying attention across variates rather than time. Each variate token represents its entire temporal series. Achieved best results on multiple multivariate benchmarks including ETT, Weather, Traffic, Solar.

key_papers.title: Forecasting at Scale
key_papers.contribution: Facebook Prophet: practical analyst-accessible forecasting using additive decomposition with piecewise linear/logistic trend, Fourier-based seasonality, and automatic changepoint detection via Laplace prior.

time_series_db.rationale: Native time-partitioning, continuous aggregates, SQL compatibility, 1M+ inserts/sec with compression

data_lake.recommended: Apache Parquet on S3/MinIO + Delta Lake 3.x
data_lake.rationale: Column-oriented, 10–50x compression, ACID transactions for schema evolution

feature_store.recommended: SQL point-in-time views in TimescaleDB (MVP/Prod v1); Feast 0.40+ (Full Platform only when <10ms online retrieval confirmed needed)
feature_store.rationale: Feast adds operational complexity; SQL views achieve correctness for batch-only use cases

streaming.recommended: Apache Kafka 3.7 + Faust 1.10 (only when streaming is confirmed customer requirement)
streaming.rationale: Most demand forecasting use cases are daily/weekly batch — defer Kafka until streaming is explicitly needed

core_framework.rationale: De facto standard; most forecasting repos (PatchTST, iTransformer, Chronos) are PyTorch-native

statistical_baselines.rationale: StatsForecast fits AutoARIMA/AutoETS on 10,000 series in seconds

gradient_boosting.rationale: M5 competition winner architecture; fastest training on tabular feature-engineered TS; native categorical support

foundation_models.rationale: HuggingFace provides unified access to Chronos, MOMENT, Moirai; TimesFM has standalone pip package

experiment_tracking.rationale: Open-source, self-hostable, Model Registry with champion/challenger management, no per-seat pricing

hpo.rationale: Async parallel trials, pruning (MedianPruner), Bayesian (TPE) and CMA-ES samplers

batch_inference.rationale: Spark JVM overhead unjustified below 50K series; Dask Python-native and sufficient for mid-market

real_time_serving.recommended: FastAPI 0.111+ (MVP + Prod v1); BentoML 1.2+ (Full Platform only)
real_time_serving.rationale: FastAPI sufficient for MVP and Prod v1; BentoML adds containerisation convenience at operator cost

model_registry.rationale: Native integration with training tracking; stage transitions with audit trail

orchestration.rationale: Battle-tested, large operator ecosystem, excellent for scheduled retraining pipelines

containerisation.recommended: Docker 26+ + Kubernetes 1.29+ (when horizontal scaling or multi-tenant isolation required)
containerisation.rationale: Docker Compose sufficient for MVP; K8s only when HPA and namespace isolation needed

monitoring.rationale: Prometheus for SLA metrics; Evidently adds ML-specific drift detection (PSI, target drift, coverage)

data_quality.rationale: Expectation suite enforces data contracts; HTML data docs; justified from day 1

mvp.scope: 1 data source, LightGBM global model, walk-forward CV, FastAPI endpoint, StatsForecast ETS baseline
mvp.notes: Optimistic assumes clean data and experienced engineer. Realistic accounts for data quality issues (70%+ probability). Data procurement time (2–6 weeks for enterprise clients) is explicitly excluded.

production_v1.scope: Multi-source ingestion, Great Expectations, LightGBM + TFT, conformal prediction, MinT reconciliation, MLflow, Airflow, Evidently monitoring, Chronos cold-start
production_v1.notes: 18 weeks is achievable if Feast is deferred (SQL views used instead) and Kafka/Spark excluded. 24–28 weeks is realistic if Feast is included or team is new to the full stack. MinT is 2.5 weeks, not 1.

full_platform.scope: All Prod v1 + multi-tenancy, foundation model fine-tuning, Next.js dashboard, auto-retraining, GPU serving
full_platform.notes: Business UI scope tends to expand — strict feature freeze required after month 5. GPU cost model must be validated before foundation model serving infrastructure is committed.

technical_feasibility.notes: All core tools production-mature with 5+ years evidence. Foundation model ecosystem tooling (monitoring, fine-tuning, cost optimisation) is immature — score deduction.

market_feasibility.notes: Strong demand across all verticals. Saturated at enterprise tier (Oracle, SAP, AWS Forecast). Foundation model zero-shot is a genuine 12–24 month differentiation window. Vietnam/SEA market 3–5 years behind US/EU — significant entry wedge.

data_feasibility.notes: Data quality highly variable. 2-year minimum history requirement disqualifies ~30% of early clients. Weekly batch ERP export (common in Vietnamese manufacturing SMEs) cannot meet daily ingestion SLA without client-side IT changes.

risk_score.notes: GPU cost at scale, walk-forward CV leakage, and Feast over-engineering are the top operational risks. All are mitigable but require organisational maturity that early-stage teams typically lack.

go_conditions: Condition 1: Customer discovery (5 interviews) to validate series count, SLA, data sovereignty, and willingness to pay before Production v1 stack selection
Condition 2: GPU cost benchmark spike (2 weeks) — test Chronos-tiny/small/base + TimesFM-200M on CPU and GPU before architecture lock
Condition 3: Walk-forward CV leakage gate implemented as CI/CD blocking test before first ML development sprint

cross_domain_connections.description: Prediction-error-based anomaly detection uses forecast residuals as the anomaly signal (|y_t - ŷ_t| > k·σ). STL decomposition residuals are the anomaly component. Conversely, anomaly-robust training (removing outliers before fitting) is critical for forecast accuracy. Both modules share drift detection infrastructure.

Demand forecasts feed inventory optimisation (newsvendor model, stochastic programming). Forecast errors propagate as stochastic uncertainty into optimisation problems. Amazon uses DeepAR forecasts as inputs to supply chain optimisation. The newsvendor optimal order quantity is F⁻¹(Cu/(Cu+Co)) — the forecast distribution F is the direct input.

Financial forecasting uses GARCH models for volatility clustering, LSTM for multi-step price series, and portfolio optimisation combines return + covariance forecasts (Black-Litterman). High-frequency trading uses order flow forecasting. The Basel III market risk framework relies on GARCH-family volatility models.

Demand forecasting is the foundational capability in S&OP. Hierarchical forecasting (SKU → category → region) is the standard architecture. Lead time forecasting enables dynamic safety stock. M5 Competition used Walmart hierarchical sales data — LightGBM won.

Short-term electricity load and renewable generation forecasting is critical for grid balancing. Probabilistic forecasting feeds stochastic unit commitment. GEFCom tracks SOTA — <2% MAPE achievable with Transformer+weather features. Multi-seasonality (daily + weekly + annual) requires MSTL.

Patient arrival forecasting drives hospital staffing. Epidemic forecasting (SIR/SEIR + ML corrections) uses TS methods. Vital sign trend forecasting in ICUs uses LSTM/Mamba for early warning. CDC FluSight competition tracks influenza forecasting SOTA annually.

LLM pre-training techniques (masked modelling, next-token prediction) have been directly adapted for TS (Chronos uses T5; MOMENT uses BERT-style masking). LLM-Time (NeurIPS 2023) shows GPT-4 achieves competitive zero-shot TS performance. Multimodal forecasting incorporating news/social media as leading indicators is an emerging cross-domain application.

use_cases: Shipment volume forecasting at DC level (daily/weekly/monthly)
Warehouse labour headcount and dock scheduling
Fleet sizing and carrier capacity booking per lane
Last-mile delivery density forecasting (parcels per zone per day)
Inventory replenishment and multi-echelon positioning
Safety stock calculation from probabilistic demand forecasts
industry_applications.special_considerations: Strong lunar calendar effects (CNY, Tet) for Asia-Pacific. Intermittent demand on long-tail lanes. Promotion calendars (11.11, Black Friday) are extreme outlier events — must be modelled as known events, not anomalies.

use_cases: SKU×Store demand forecasting for inventory planning
Hierarchical category/region/national forecasting with MinT reconciliation
New product launch cold-start forecasting via foundation models
Price elasticity and promotion lift modelling
Markdown and clearance quantity optimisation
industry_applications.target_accuracy: MAPE < 10–15% at SKU level; PI coverage 78–82% for 80% nominal
industry_applications.special_considerations: Zero-inflated demand for slow-movers and new SKUs — use Tweedie loss. Promotion confounding: without promotion flag as feature, lag features carry misleading signal during heavy promotion periods.

use_cases: Short-term electricity load forecasting (1h–48h) for grid balancing
Solar and wind generation forecasting for renewable integration
Price forecasting for energy trading
HVAC and building energy consumption forecasting
industry_applications.special_considerations: Multiple seasonality periods (hourly + daily + weekly + annual) require MSTL. Probabilistic forecasting (quantile stacks) feeds stochastic unit commitment. GEFCom competition benchmark tracks SOTA.

use_cases: Returns and price series forecasting
Volatility forecasting (GARCH family) for risk management
Portfolio return and covariance forecasting
Credit default risk early warning
industry_applications.target_accuracy: MAPE < 5–8%; Sharpe ratio improvement as business metric
industry_applications.special_considerations: Near-efficient markets, heavy-tailed distributions, regime changes. The Basel III market risk framework requires GARCH-class volatility models. HIPAA/PDPA do not apply but MiFID II data handling requirements do in EU.

use_cases: Crop yield forecasting for supply planning
Weather-adjusted demand forecasting for agricultural inputs
Price forecasting for commodity hedging
industry_applications.special_considerations: Strong weather dependency. Irregular growing seasons. Data sparsity for smallholder farms.

risks.name: GPU Cost Spiral
risks.description: Foundation model GPU inference costs compound non-linearly as client series counts grow. Without a cost model integrated into client pricing, the platform may be revenue-positive but margin-negative at scale.
risks.mitigation: GPU cost benchmark spike before Prod v1 architecture lock. Limit foundation models to cold-start path (< 60 obs). Pre-compute nightly; never serve synchronously. Evaluate Chronos-tiny as default (15% CRPS penalty acceptable for cold-start).

risks.name: Walk-Forward CV Leakage — Silent Quality Corruption
risks.description: Incorrect gap configuration in walk-forward CV produces training MASE ~0.78 that degrades to ~0.95 in production. Detection requires 4–8 weeks of live monitoring. Client trust is destroyed when the gap is discovered.
risks.mitigation: CI/CD blocking gate: assert gap >= max(forecast_horizon, max_lag_features). Canary leakage test. Code review checklist for all feature engineering. Compare training-time vs live production MASE on first client deployment.

risks.name: Feast Feature Store Over-Engineering
risks.description: Feast consistently causes 2–4x timeline overruns on first implementations. Including Feast in Production v1 critical path converts the 18-week timeline to 24–28 weeks.
risks.mitigation: Implement features as point-in-time correct SQL views in TimescaleDB for Prod v1. Introduce Feast only in Full Platform phase, only when sub-10ms online feature retrieval is a confirmed requirement.

risks.name: Hyperscaler Commoditisation of Foundation Models
risks.description: AWS, Google Cloud, and Nixtla TimeGPT are direct threats. The 12–24 month window before zero-shot foundation models are a $10/month managed service is narrow.
risks.mitigation: Shift primary moat to MLOps pipeline depth (monitoring, explainability, hierarchical reconciliation, ERP integration) rather than model quality. Establish 3 reference clients with documented ROI within 9 months.

risks.name: Data Availability Barrier for Early Clients
risks.description: ~30% of potential early clients lack the 2-year minimum history for trained global models. Vietnamese manufacturing SMEs on weekly ERP batch cadence cannot meet the daily ingestion SLA without 4–12 weeks of client-side IT work.
risks.mitigation: Foundation model cold-start path mitigates history requirement (with 15–20% MASE degradation caveat). Offer weekly-frequency forecasting tier for clients on weekly batch cadence. Qualify data maturity in first client discovery conversation.

data_sources: R-MLE, R-DE, R-DA, R-BE, R-DO, R-QA, R-D06, R-SA, R-FE Practitioner Notes — B01 — 2026-03-30
